question: Provide a comprehensive list of all personal information and personal data elements and categories defined across US privacy regulations in the corpus, including sensitive personal information and special categories.
answer:
COMPREHENSIVE LIST OF PERSONAL INFORMATION AND PERSONAL DATA ELEMENTS ACROSS US PRIVACY REGULATIONS

The retrieved regulations define both general personal data/information and specific sensitive categories. Below is an organized compilation across all jurisdictions represented in the corpus.

────────────────────────────────────────────
I. GENERAL PERSONAL DATA / PERSONAL INFORMATION
────────────────────────────────────────────

Most state laws share a broad baseline definition. "Personal data" means any information that is linked or reasonably linkable to an identified or identifiable natural person [MN MCDPA §(p)][NJDPA]. Oregon's Consumer Privacy Act similarly defines "personal data" as data, derived data, or any unique identifier linked to or reasonably linkable to a consumer or to a device linked to one or more consumers in a household [OCPA 646A.572].

Oregon's breach-notification statute defines "personal information" more specifically as a consumer's first name or first initial and last name in combination with one or more of the following data elements (when not rendered unusable by encryption or redaction) [OCPA ORS]:

• Social Security number
• Driver license number or state identification card number issued by the Department of Transportation
• Passport number or other U.S.-government-issued identification number
• Financial account number, credit card number, or debit card number, combined with any required security code, access code, or password permitting access to a financial account, or any other information that would permit such access
• Data from automatic measurements of a consumer's physical characteristics (e.g., fingerprint, retina, or iris image) used to authenticate identity in a financial or other transaction
• Health insurance policy number or health insurance subscriber identification number combined with any other unique identifier used by a health insurer
• Any information about a consumer's medical history or mental or physical condition, or about a health care professional's medical diagnosis or treatment of the consumer
• A username or other means of identifying a consumer for account access, together with any authentication method necessary to access the account
• Any of the above data elements alone (without name) if not rendered unusable and if they would enable identity theft [OCPA ORS §12(a)(A)–(C)]

────────────────────────────────────────────
II. SENSITIVE DATA / SPECIAL CATEGORIES
────────────────────────────────────────────

All four state consumer privacy laws in the corpus define a "sensitive data" category subject to heightened requirements. The categories overlap substantially but differ in some details.

A. OREGON (OCPA)
Sensitive data means personal data that [OCPA 646A.572 §18(a)]:
• Reveals a consumer's racial or ethnic background, national origin, religious beliefs, mental or physical condition or diagnosis, sexual orientation, status as transgender or nonbinary, status as a victim of crime, or citizenship or immigration status
• Is a child's personal data
• Accurately identifies within a radius of 1,750 feet a consumer's present or past location, or the present or past location of a linked device (via GPS or similar technology)
• Is genetic or biometric data

B. KENTUCKY (KCDPA)
Sensitive data means [KCDPA §28]:
• Personal data indicating racial or ethnic origin, religious beliefs, mental or physical health diagnosis, sexual orientation, or citizenship or immigration status
• Genetic or biometric data processed for the purpose of uniquely identifying a specific natural person
• Personal data collected from a known child
• Precise geolocation data

C. MINNESOTA (MN MCDPA)
Sensitive data means [MN MCDPA §(v)]:
• Personal data revealing racial or ethnic origin, religious beliefs, mental or physical health condition or diagnosis, sexual orientation, or citizenship or immigration status
• Biometric data or genetic information processed for the purpose of uniquely identifying an individual
• Personal data of a known child (a person under 13 where the controller has actual knowledge or willfully disregards the person's age)
• Specific geolocation data

D. NEW JERSEY (NJDPA)
Sensitive data means personal data revealing [NJDPA]:
• Racial or ethnic origin
• Religious beliefs
• Mental or physical health condition, treatment, or diagnosis
• Financial information, including a consumer's account number, account log-in, financial account, or credit or debit card number combined with any required security code, access code, or password permitting access to a financial account (NOTE: New Jersey uniquely includes financial information as sensitive data, unlike the other three states)
• Sex life or sexual orientation
• Citizenship or immigration status
• Status as transgender or non-binary
• Genetic or biometric data (that may be used to uniquely identify an individual — clause is truncated in retrieved text)
• Precise geolocation data (defined as within a radius of 1,750 feet, derived from GPS-level coordinates or other mechanisms) [NJDPA]

────────────────────────────────────────────
III. KEY DEFINITIONAL DIFFERENCES ACROSS JURISDICTIONS
────────────────────────────────────────────

Several notable differences exist:

1. Financial information: Only New Jersey explicitly includes financial account credentials (account numbers, card numbers with security codes) as sensitive data [NJDPA]. Oregon's breach-notification statute covers these under its personal information definition [OCPA ORS §12(a)(A)(iv)], but not within sensitive data per the privacy act.

2. Victim status / national origin: Oregon uniquely includes "status as a victim of crime" and "national origin" as sensitive data categories [OCPA §18(a)(A)]. Kentucky and Minnesota do not.

3. Transgender/non-binary status: Oregon [OCPA §18(a)(A)] and New Jersey [NJDPA] explicitly call out transgender or nonbinary status. Kentucky and Minnesota do not list it separately, though sexual orientation is covered by all four.

4. Genetic information as standalone: Minnesota defines "genetic information" by cross-reference to its own state statute (section 13.386, subdivision 1) [MN MCDPA §(m)], treating it as a separately defined term in addition to its inclusion in sensitive data.

5. Geolocation precision: Oregon uses a 1,750-foot radius standard [OCPA §18(a)(C)]; New Jersey uses the same standard [NJDPA]; Kentucky uses the term "precise geolocation data" without specifying a radius [KCDPA §28(d)]; Minnesota uses "specific geolocation data" [MN MCDPA §(v)(4)].

6. Children's data: All four laws include personal data of children as sensitive, but Minnesota defines "known child" specifically as under 13 years of age where the controller has actual knowledge or willfully disregards the age [MN MCDPA §(o)]. The other laws use "known child" without an identical statutory gloss in the retrieved text.

────────────────────────────────────────────
SOURCES
────────────────────────────────────────────

[OCPA] — Oregon Consumer Privacy Act (ORS 646A.570 et seq.) and Oregon's personal information/breach statute (ORS 646A.602 et seq.)
[KCDPA] — Kentucky Consumer Data Protection Act
[MN MCDPA] — Minnesota Consumer Data Privacy Act
[NJDPA] — New Jersey Data Privacy Act (P.L. 2023, Chapter 266)
